question: Compare the NuScale RCS flow (0 RCPs, Table 1.3-1) with the primary flow rates in Table 5.1-2.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It lists the relevant diagrams and legends used for both I&C and piping and instrumentation diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0558.title: DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0: Radiation Protection Design FeaturesII.9 RG 8.15
nuscale_ch01_0558.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory guides related to radiation protection design features, as outlined in DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0. It details whether each regulatory guide is applicable, fully or partially conformed to, or not applicable, with references to supporting sections and tables for further information.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across different power levels by establishing steady-state parameters for both primary and secondary systems, such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures. These parameters are calculated using thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand, with uncertainties and design margins considered to ensure efficient operation and safety. The design supports natural circulation with low pressure losses, and uncertainties like SG tube plugging do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in size, output, and system components, showing that NuScale uses smaller, simpler, and more integrated systems compared to conventional PWRs. Tables provide side-by-side comparisons of key parameters and safety systems for both designs.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems, and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.

nuscale_ch01_0741.title: SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, Rev 2: Startup of an Inactive Loop or Recirculation Loop at an Incorrect Temperature, and Flow Controller Malfunction Causing an Increase in BWR Core Flow RateII.A RCS and MSS Pressures
nuscale_ch01_0741.summary: The section explains that the regulatory guidance in SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5 regarding startup of an inactive loop or recirculation loop at an incorrect temperature, and flow controller malfunction causing increased BWR core flow rate, is not applicable to the NuScale reactor design because it does not use reactor coolant pumps or forced coolant flow. Instead, NuScale addresses similar postulated startup reactivity accidents under DSRS 15.4.6, which covers inadvertent decreases in boron concentration in the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s passive decay heat removal system, which uses two independent trains, each capable of removing 100% of the decay heat via natural circulation and passive condensers immersed in the reactor pool. In case of a steam generator tube failure or loss of AC power, the system isolates the affected components and continues cooling the reactor by transferring heat to a large, stainless steel-lined ultimate heat sink pool, ensuring safe heat removal even during accidents.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0743.title: SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, Rev 2: Startup of an Inactive Loop or Recirculation Loop at an Incorrect Temperature, and Flow Controller Malfunction Causing an Increase in BWR Core Flow RateII.C Events of Moderate Frequency
nuscale_ch01_0743.summary: This section explains that the regulatory guidance in SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, which addresses startup of inactive loops or flow controller malfunctions in PWR and BWR designs, does not apply to the NuScale reactor because it does not use reactor coolant pumps or forced coolant flow. Instead, any relevant postulated startup reactivity accidents for NuScale are addressed under DSRS 15.4.6, which covers inadvertent decreases in boron concentration.

edges.description: Section A provides detailed design drawings and figures (including instrumentation and control diagrams) that visually clarify system configurations such as RCS flow, while Section B documents the conformance of these design features to specific regulatory criteria for radiation protection, thus Section A's technical details support the regulatory claims made in Section B.

Section A provides detailed diagrams and figures that visually represent system components and flows, which can be used to interpret and verify the primary flow rates listed as steady-state values in Section B's system evaluation tables.

Section B (Table 1.3-1) provides the nominal RCS flow values for NuScale (0 RCPs), which are the design parameters that should be compared against the primary flow rates listed in Table 5.1-2, while Section A provides the supporting drawings and figures that visually clarify the system design referenced in Section B.

Section A references regulatory guidance and conformance criteria for radiation protection design features, while Section B provides system evaluation details including primary flow rates, thus Section B provides the technical design results (primary flow rates) that must be compared against the regulatory requirements referenced in Section A.

Section A discusses the establishment of steady state primary flow rates as a function of reactor power, while Section B provides the nominal RCS flow values (including 0 RCPs) in Table 1.3-1 for comparison with other facilities, thus Section A's analysis relies on or references the specific flow values presented in Section B.

Section A describes operational procedures and system component states relevant to RCS flow, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and figures that visually clarify and support the textual descriptions in Section A, connecting operational context with design documentation.

Section A describes operational steps and system configurations affecting RCS flow, while Section B discusses how steady-state flow parameters, such as those in Table 5.1-2, are established and evaluated for optimal plant performance, connecting the operational context to the quantitative flow values.

Section A references regulatory criteria and design limits for RCS flow and fuel safety, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and tables (including Table 1.3-1) that specify the actual NuScale RCS flow values, thus Section A's analysis relies on the data presented in Section B.

Section A references specific RCS flow conditions (0 RCPs, Table 1.3-1) that should be compared to the primary flow rates established in Section B's Table 5.1-2, connecting them through the design values for RCS flow under different operational scenarios.

Section A provides operational details and system preparation steps for the NuScale RCS, while Section B outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for RCS and MSS pressures, connecting them through the requirement that operational procedures (A) must satisfy the regulatory limits and criteria (B) for primary flow rates.

Section A describes the functional operation and safety role of the Chemical and Volume Control System during specific events, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify and support the system descriptions in Section A.

Section A describes the decay heat removal system's capability to cool the RCS via natural circulation, while Section B discusses how steady-state primary flow rates (including those with 0 RCPs) are established as a function of reactor power, connecting both sections through the operational performance and flow characteristics of the RCS under different conditions.

Section A provides a general design description stating that the NuScale RCS relies on natural circulation without reactor coolant pumps, which is the operational context for the RCS flow values presented in Section B's detailed drawings and tables.

Section A describes the design basis of the NuScale RCS, specifically noting reliance on natural circulation without reactor coolant pumps, which directly informs the system evaluation in Section B where steady state primary flow rates (as in Table 5.1-2) are established based on this design.

Section A explains that certain SRP guidance is not applicable to NuScale due to the absence of reactor coolant pumps, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and tables (such as Table 1.3-1) that illustrate the actual RCS flow configuration without pumps, thus supporting Section A's claim.

Section A explains that SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5 guidance on RCS flow is not applicable to NuScale due to the absence of reactor coolant pumps, while Section B discusses how NuScale establishes steady-state primary flow rates as part of its system evaluation, connecting both sections through the design choice of natural circulation (no RCPs) and its impact on flow rate parameters.

Section A describes the decay heat removal system's reliance on natural circulation for cooling, which aligns with Section B's summary that the NuScale RCS operates solely on natural circulation without reactor coolant pumps, connecting both sections through the design choice of natural circulation flow.

Section B explains that SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5 guidance is not applicable to NuScale because it lacks reactor coolant pumps, which is consistent with Section A's description of a natural circulation RCS without pumps.

Section A describes the NuScale RCS design as relying solely on natural circulation without reactor coolant pumps, while Section B explains that certain regulatory criteria are not applicable to NuScale because it lacks reactor coolant pumps, directly referencing the design described in Section A.
